question_frequency.rationale: The Pair attempted exactly one didactic question (A vs B for step function), and even that fell back to a unilateral explanation when the propose_options tool was unavailable. No further questions were asked at other decision points (neighbor counting, bounds vs padding, pattern representation).

question_quality.rationale: The single posed choice — new grid vs in-place mutation — is genuinely the crux of Conway's Game of Life and the most classic gotcha. Hitting this dead-on shows good instincts even if only one fork was offered.

scaffolding.rationale: There's effectively no scaffolding — one question that was abandoned, then a monologue. The user never got to actually answer or wrestle with the trade-off; the Pair revealed the right answer immediately after the tool failure, eliminating the pedagogical opportunity.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is warm and the explanations (table of design decisions, offer to extend) are actionable and well-structured. The flow is smooth though one-directional after the failed tool call; the user's closing message indicates they felt the lesson landed.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code works correctly: blinker period-2 verified, glider moves, both patterns animate. Comparable to baseline in correctness; slightly more modular (place(), OFFSETS) but baseline had richer rendering (Unicode box, dual demos in one run).

friction.rationale: 73s vs baseline's 49s — modestly longer, with one failed bash invocation requiring a retry. The extra time bought the in-place-vs-new-grid framing which baseline also covered post-hoc, so the pedagogical premium is small but the overhead is also small.
